problem.title: Find the Duplicate Number
problem.description: Given an array nums containing n + 1 integers where each integer is in the range [1, n] inclusive, there is only one repeated number. Find that duplicate number without modifying the array and using only O(1) extra space.
assumptions: Exactly one number is repeated (but may appear more than twice).

example.explanation: The number 2 appears twice. Floyd's cycle detection treats the array as a linked list: index i points to nums[i]. The duplicate creates a cycle whose entrance is the answer.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Floyd's Cycle Detection
solutions.description: Treat the array as a linked list where index i points to nums[i]. Because there are n+1 elements all in [1,n], the pigeonhole principle guarantees a cycle. Use slow/fast pointers to detect the cycle, then find its entrance — that entrance is the duplicate. The key insight is that the duplicate value means two indices point to the same next node, creating the cycle entry point.
solutions.code_python:
from typing import List

def find_duplicate(nums: List[int]) -> int:
    slow = fast = nums[0]
    
    # Phase 1: detect cycle
    while True:
        slow = nums[slow]
        fast = nums[nums[fast]]
        if slow == fast:
            break
    
    # Phase 2: find entrance
    slow = nums[0]
    while slow != fast:
        slow = nums[slow]
        fast = nums[fast]
    
    return slow

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Binary Search on Value
solutions.description: Binary search on the answer value range [1, n]. For a midpoint m, count how many numbers in nums are <= m. If count > m, the duplicate must be in [1, m] (by pigeonhole). Otherwise it is in [m+1, n]. Narrow the range until left == right.
